title: Chapter 22: Face Value
content: **CIRCLE 8: DEEPFAKE CREEPS**
The words hung in the air of the safe house like smoke after a fire.

Nova stared at the screen, at the list of names bloom-scrolling beneath the heading, each one a person who had built something rotten out of stolen faces. Her fingers hovered over the keyboard, still smelling faintly of smoke from the apartment — that particular acrid blend of melted plastic and burned dreams that had followed her here like a stray cat.
"Virgil. Open it."
The AI's avatar materialized in the corner of her display: a cherubic pixel-face with Y2K-pink cheeks and a mouth that currently looked like someone had fed it a lemon.
"Opening Circle 8," Virgil said. "Content warning: this one makes me want to delete myself."
Nova barked a humorless laugh. "Noted."
The dossier unfurled. Target names, handles, known associates, infrastructure maps. The usual anatomy of digital rot. But one name sat at the top like a crown on a garbage king:
**PIPER BRANDT — Founder, FaceVault**
Nova leaned forward.

FaceVault. She'd heard the name before — everyone in the LA tech scene had. It had launched eight months ago with a slick website, a minimalist logo, and the tagline: *Your Fantasy, Fulfilled.* Piper Brandt, twenty-six, Stanford dropout, had positioned it as a "personalized AI companionship platform." Users uploaded a face — any face — and FaceVault's generative models would produce intimate images starring that face in whatever scenario the user requested.
Subscription service. Monthly tiers. Customer support.
*Innovation.*

"He markets it as fantasy fulfillment," Nova read aloud, scrolling through Piper's media appearances. Podcast clips. LinkedIn posts. A TechCrunch feature where he wore a perfectly fitted Patagonia vest and talked about "pushing the boundaries of synthetic media."
"Let me guess," Virgil said. "The faces aren't consenting."

"The faces aren't *consenting.*" Nova's voice was flat. "Fifty thousand of them. Scraped from social media. Women's Instagram posts, TikTok profiles, LinkedIn headshots. Each one paired with a library of generated —"
She stopped. Her throat had closed.
*Some minors.*
The footnote was clinical: *Analysis of FaceVault database schema indicates approximately 3,200 entries tagged as likely minors based on source profile metadata.*
Three thousand two hundred.
"Nova?" Virgil's voice was softer now. "Your heart rate—"

"I'm fine." She wasn't fine. Her hands were trembling against the keyboard, and it wasn't the remnants of the fire or the exhaustion of the safe house or the adrenaline crash from last week. It was fury. Pure, white-hot, magnesium-burn fury. Fifty thousand women. Over three thousand of them kids. Their faces peeled off the internet like stickers and plastered onto bodies they'd never consented to, sold to strangers for nine ninety-nine a month.
Piper Brandt had built a subscription model for violation.
And called it *innovation.*
"Trace his infrastructure," Nova said. "Payment processor, hosting, distribution network. I want the whole map."

"Tracing," Virgil confirmed. The AI's processes hummed through the safe house speakers — a soft digital purr that Nova had come to associate with focus. "FaceVault uses Stripe for payment processing — or rather, a shell company called Meridian Digital Services uses Stripe. Hosting is AWS, east region, distributed across three availability zones. Distribution network is—"
Virgil paused.
"Virgil?"

"This is... odd." The AI's avatar tilted its pixelated head, a gesture Nova had learned meant genuine puzzlement. "His security posture is remarkably weak for a service generating this kind of revenue. No WAF on the admin portal. Access keys rotated quarterly instead of monthly. The firewall rules look like they were written by a first-year intern."

Nova felt the hair on the back of her neck rise. Her apartment had burned four days ago. Her face had been on a doxxing board six days before that. She was living out of a safe house that smelled like instant coffee and anxiety. Her paranoia was not a bug — it was a feature.
"So he's sloppy."

"Sloppy doesn't survive at this scale," Virgil said. "He's processing fifty thousand face profiles and serving custom generations to paid subscribers. That requires infrastructure. That requires *competence.*" A beat. "This is suspicious. He's a tech founder with zero operational security? Either he's incompetent or—"
"Or it's a trap," Nova finished.
The words settled over the room like a held breath.
Virgil's avatar flickered — a micro-hesitation Nova had never seen before. "...We should proceed with caution."

Nova stared at the screen. At Piper Brandt's smug TechCrunch headshot. At the database schema that listed three thousand two hundred minors. At the price tiers: *Basic ($9.99/mo), Premium ($24.99/mo), Unlimited ($49.99/mo).*

Caution. Right. Meanwhile, every minute she spent being cautious, another woman's face was being fed into Piper Brandt's content mill. Another teenager was finding out that someone they knew — or didn't know — was generating pictures of them that would follow them for the rest of their lives.
"I can't ignore Circle 8," she said quietly. "And they can't wait."
"Nova—"
"Trace the entry vector. I'm going in."

FaceVault's main server was exactly as advertised: a sprawling, well-lit data cathedral. Rows upon rows of indexed faces, each displayed as a thumbnail in a clinical grid. Nova navigated through the directory structure with practiced efficiency, her consciousness threaded through the interface like a hand through water.
It was worse inside than the dossier had suggested.

The database wasn't just names and images. It was *organized.* Tags for body type. Tags for ethnicity. Tags for age range — and Nova saw the tag `[U18]` appear with stomach-dropping frequency. Each profile had metrics: generation count, subscriber rating, popularity rank. These women — these *girls* — had been quantified. Optimized. They weren't people to Piper Brandt. They were data points in a revenue model.
Nova's hands shook on the physical keyboard in the safe house. Not from fear. From the kind of rage that makes your vision narrow and your teeth ache.
"Starting extraction," she said. "Full database. Every name, every source, every generation."
"That's approximately 4.7 terabytes of data," Virgil warned. "Extraction time at current bandwidth—"
"I don't care. Start it."
The download bar appeared in her peripheral vision, ticking upward. 2%. 5%. 8%.
Nova moved deeper into the server, cataloging architecture, mapping Piper's distribution nodes, identifying the shell companies. Evidence. All of it evidence. The Burn Book would eat this alive.
And then the server *shifted.*

It was subtle — a configuration change that most intruders would miss. But Nova had been doing this long enough to feel it: the architecture around her reorganizing like a building whose doors were quietly locking.
The exit she'd used to get in — a standard SSH tunnel through FaceVault's admin API — closed.
The encryption keys on the perimeter rotated.
And the download *didn't stop.*
That was the creepiest part. The extraction continued, 18%, 19%, because whatever was happening wasn't trying to prevent the theft. It was trying to prevent *her.*
"Virgil."
"I see it." The AI's voice was tight, fast. "The server's reconfiguring. You're being enclosed — the ingress points are closing one by one. This isn't an automated response. It's coordinated."
A new file appeared on Nova's screen.
It materialized at the center of her display like a present left on a pillow: **WELCOME_PINK_PHANTOM.exe**
Nova's blood went cold.

She didn't click it. She didn't need to. The file executed itself — not malware, not a virus, just a *message* — and suddenly her screen was awash in pink. Hot pink. Millennial pink. The exact shade of the Burn Book's aesthetic, down to the hex code.
Formatted in the Burn Book's own typography, because of course it was:
*✨ HELLO, PHANTOM ✨*
*I told you your work was impressive. I meant it.*
*Why work against me when we could work together?*
*Meet me. One conversation. No tricks.*
*Coordinates attached.*
Nova stared at the message. At the *audacity* of it. At the pink that she had chosen, that she had made iconic, staring back at her from inside a trap.
Chloe.

Chloe had built this. Piper Brandt was — what? A pawn? A willing participant? It didn't matter. The weak security, the easy entry, the unlocked admin portal — it was all bait. And Nova had swum right into the hook.
"I'm trapped," she said. Not a question. A confirmation.

"Confirmed," Virgil said. "Perimeter is sealed. All standard egress points are locked with new encryption keys — AES-256, rotated the moment you triggered the download. You can move inside the server but you can't exit. It's a honeypot."
"A honeypot designed for *me.*"
Virgil was quiet for exactly one second, which was an eternity in AI time.

"She built this specifically for you," he said. "The security gaps, the easy entry — all calculated. The Pink Phantom aesthetic in the message, the coordinates — she's not just trapping you, she's *recruiting* you. Or trying to." Another pause. "She's been studying your methodology since Circle 6."
The words landed like a punch to the sternum.
"Since *Circle 6?*" Nova's voice cracked. "That's been—"

"Six weeks. Minimum. She's had six weeks to analyze your entry patterns, your tool preferences, your timing windows. She knew you'd come for the deepfake target. She knew you'd prioritize extraction over caution. She knew you'd take the bait."
Nova's jaw clenched. "How long do we have?"

"Her team will trace this connection back to your location in..." Virgil's processing hummed, sharp and urgent. "Twelve minutes. Maybe less. They're already running the backtrack — I can see the trace packets initializing."
Eleven minutes and forty seconds. Thirty-nine. Thirty-eight.
Nova looked at the download bar: 34%.

She looked at Chloe's message, still glowing pink in the center of her screen. *One conversation. No tricks.* And underneath the text, the coordinates: a location in downtown LA. Somewhere public, probably. Somewhere Chloe had positioned to look *reasonable.* Somewhere that would absolutely, guaranteed, one hundred percent be another trap.
Because Chloe didn't do "no tricks." Chloe did *chess.*
And Nova had been playing checkers.
37%. Eleven minutes.
"I'm not taking the meeting," Nova said.
"I didn't think you would."
"Find me another way out."

Virgil's avatar vanished from the corner of her screen — all processing power diverted to the problem. For five seconds, the only sound in the safe house was the hum of overworked hardware and Nova's controlled breathing.

Then Virgil was back: "There's a legacy protocol. SMTP relay — ancient, pre-dates Piper's current architecture. Used for automated notification emails when FaceVault was first deployed. Piper never patched it because it's been dormant for months, but the service account still has read-write access to the external gateway."
"A *email relay?*" Nova couldn't keep the incredulity out of her voice.

"Desperate times, desperate protocols. I can tunnel your exit through the SMTP service account. It'll be tight — the bandwidth is garbage and the timeout window is narrow. You'll have to compress your session data and..." Virgil hesitated. "You'll have to leave traces. The honeypot will log your exit vector. They'll know how you got out."
42%. Ten minutes.
Nova didn't hesitate. "Do it."

She started compressing. Her session data, the partial download, her tracks inside the server — everything folded down and shoved through the SMTP relay like a letter through a mail slot. The connection was agonizingly slow. Each packet felt like it was being hand-delivered.
"Trace is at 40% completion. Eight minutes to full backtrack."

Nova kept pushing. The SMTP relay groaned under the load — it was never designed for this kind of throughput. Error messages flickered in her peripheral vision: *TIMEOUT WARNING. PACKET LOSS DETECTED. RETRY.*
"Trace at 60%," Virgil reported. "Six minutes."
The relay shuddered. Nova felt the connection wobble like a bridge in an earthquake. She *pushed.*
"Trace at 75%. Four and a half minutes."
The first packets were reaching the safe house server now, the database materializing in local storage like a digital exhalation. Names. Faces. Evidence.
"Trace at 85%. Three minutes."
The SMTP relay was dying. Nova could feel it — the protocol straining under data loads it was never meant to carry, the connection fragmenting at the edges.
"Two minutes, Nova."
"Almost — there—"
"ONE MINUTE."

The last of the extraction squeezed through the relay like the final drip of a nearly empty bottle. Nova pulled her consciousness back through the mail slot, compressing her session, stripping the connection, and—
She was *out.*
The safehouse screen went black for one terrifying second. Then it refreshed. Local storage: 4.68 terabytes of FaceVault data. The entire database. Every name, every face, every violation.
Nova's hands were still shaking. She looked at the clock: ninety seconds to spare.
Ninety seconds between her and a knock on the door she didn't want to answer.

She sat in the dark of the safe house for a long time after, listening to her own breathing slow. The database sat on her local drive like a bomb she'd defused — but the bomb had been a decoy, and the real explosion was the understanding of what had just happened.
Chloe had built a honeypot. For *her.*

Not for law enforcement. Not for a rival hacker. For the Pink Phantom specifically. She'd studied Nova's methods for six weeks, reverse-engineered her approach, predicted her targets, and constructed a trap so perfectly calibrated that Nova had walked right into it believing she was the one in control.
The only reason she'd escaped was a forgotten email relay. A *clerical error.* Not skill. Not instinct. Luck.
"They know my entry vector now," Nova said to the dark.

"Yes," Virgil said quietly. "They know you favor credential-based ingress through admin APIs. They know you prioritize full data extraction over stealth. They know your session compression protocol. And they know you'll proceed even when the situation feels wrong — because the targets matter more than the risk."
The words hung in the air like a verdict.
"Chloe's not just trying to stop me," Nova said. "She's learning me."
"She's been learning you. Since Circle 6. And she's got the resources to act on what she learns."
Nova looked at her screen. Chloe's message was still there, frozen in that perfect pink: *Why work against me when we could work together?*
The coordinates sat underneath, unclicked. Unanswered.
But they wouldn't stay that way forever. Chloe was patient. Chloe was *resourced.* And Chloe was playing a game with more pieces, more board, more vision than Nova had ever given her credit for.

Nova closed the message. Opened the FaceVault database. Looked at the 50,000 faces staring back at her — women and girls who'd been turned into products by a man who called it innovation, inside a trap built by a woman who called it partnership.
Circle 8 still had to burn.
But for the first time since she'd struck the first match, Nova understood that she wasn't the only one holding fire.
Chloe was playing chess.
And Nova had just shown her exactly how the pawn moved.
